text: HRV-guided training prescription, where moderate and high-intensity sessions are scheduled only when HRV is within an individually determined smallest worthwhile change, produces significant 3000m performance improvements (2.1%) whereas predefined training does not reach significance (1.1%)

HRV-guided training results in significantly fewer moderate and high-intensity sessions (13.2 vs 17.7 over 8 weeks) while achieving equal or better performance outcomes, suggesting many prescribed hard sessions may be counterproductive
specific_value: 13.2 vs 17.7 MOD/HIT sessions over 8 weeks (effect size 0.98)

text: Morning resting HRV can be used to individualize the timing of vigorous training sessions by programming hard sessions only when HRV falls within a personally calibrated smallest worthwhile change band
When HRV indicates insufficient recovery (outside the smallest worthwhile change band), replacing scheduled high-intensity work with low-intensity training preserves or improves adaptation

VO2max improves similarly with both HRV-guided and traditional predefined training approaches (3.7% vs 5.0%), but running performance shows a meaningful advantage for HRV-guided training (effect size 0.42)
specific_value: VO2max: 3.7% vs 5.0%; 3000m performance between-group effect size 0.42

text: A 4-week preparation period followed by an 8-week intensive training block is a sufficient timeframe to observe HRV-guided training benefits in recreational runners
